title: Home Page — since it a sales page, book items are priced aggresively,
hypothesis: since it a sales page, book items are priced aggresively, but because of low visibility and low discoverablity users dont get to see this page, increasing the discoverability of this category page, lowering conversion from this category. increasing visibility should increase view_items, and increase purchases from this category.
problem_statement: extra-discount-book category page is hidden away, not easily discoverable

metrics.primary:
last 3 months: nov-dec-jan

page_views : was - 0.043%
active_users : was - 0.13%

ATC rate: 25.23% 
CR rate: 1.8%

purchase count: 198
daily purchase: 2.15 / day

revenue_impact:
comparing last 3 months : nov-dec-jan
from this section

prev: 92 days
total orders = 198
daily orders = 2.15 / day
AOV = 908.94

experiment : 26 days
total orders = 213
daily orders = 8.19 / day
AOV = 1063.25

total gain compared to last 3 months = 
(8.19-2.15)*26*1082.38 = 1,66,972.78 BDT
 - looks alot inflated, as this section has alot of free ebooks + lower cost books, do not reflect our AOV
- purchase count for nov-dec might look lower due to the existing purchase_event bug, which have been solved
- but purchase did increase from this section.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes:
current results : 
page_views : currently - 0.06%
active_users : currently - 0.25%

ATC rate: 21.92% 
CR rate: 4.5%

purchase count: 213
daily purchase: 10.46 / day
[April] Monthly orders and AOV metrics exclude February to prevent data skew from peak book sales. | Performance remained positive despite significant dips in book orders surrounding Eid. | Sold-count was slightly lower; need to investigate root cause.
[May] Section traffic nearly doubled in May (10,487 views vs. the ~5,400/month pre-ship baseline) as the new nav placement compounded reach. | Browse-to-product conversion lifted and held: view_item rose from 49.8% pre-ship (March) to 63–65% across April and May. | Purchase rate held steady at ~3% of section views — purchase volume grew with traffic (150 → 322), though ATC and purchase conversion softened slightly from April's peak.

remarks: clear increase in user engagement for this category. total conversion also increased. · Report pill: ✓ Holding · Report months: April+May · April status: ✓ Shipped A
potential_outcome: increase "extra-discount-books" page_views and view_items, should also improve purchase_count as visibility increase.

problem_statement: some products in quick-deal section show their brand information as "non-brand", app doesn't have brand information for quick-deal product cards.

title: Foreign book price-category — section redesign

results.notes:
[April] High customer engagement in 'Under 99' and 'Free Shipping' categories. (almost x4 of the other 2 categories) These can be effect Book Sales Categories. | Sections has recently been removed | Archive the winning variant for future re-introduction.
[May] High engagement in the 'Under 99' and 'Free Shipping' categories (~×4 the other two) — strong signal for clearance-style merchandising. | Despite positive results, the section was removed from the homepage during the homepage redesign — the lift is no longer being realised. | Winning Light variant archived for future re-introduction if the section returns.

remarks: Report months: April+May · April status: ✓ Ship Light

title: QuickDeal Badge — Related Product Section
hypothesis: Shipped to the related-product section last month — a QuickDeal badge flags active deals at the point of discovery. Tracking the post-ship desktop funnel from badge click through to purchase.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes:
[April] Tracking Dilution: Users are correctly bucketed into variants, but the experiment is "diluted" when the viewed product lacks a Quick-Deal item in the first pagination. This leads to users being in a variant without actually experiencing the feature. | Next Step: Launch a new experiment that re-ranks related products to force "Quick-Deal" items into the first pagination. This will maximize visibility and minimize data noise from bucketed users who never see the treatment.
[May] The badge pulled 2,221 desktop clicks into the funnel; ~1 in 4 (25.6%) reached add-to-cart and 4.9% converted to purchase. | High-value traffic: AOV of ৳947.05 is well above site average, so the 108 purchases carry an outsized ~৳1.02L in attributed revenue. | Biggest drop-off is at the click → ATC step (74.4% abandon). No control arm was captured, so revenue is gross-attributed, not incremental lift — add a holdout to quantify true impact.

remarks: Report pill: Measured · Report months: April+May · April status: ✓ Ship B

title: You-are-saving — font size optimisation
hypothesis: A more prominent savings amount nudges purchase intent. The current font size seems too small for our avg users. Bigger font should increase ATC and purchase rate.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: ✓ Ship A · Report months: April

title: Checkout summary — clarity redesign
hypothesis: On our mobile shipping page, the checkout summary was hidden behind a toggle, showing only the total amount, which might not be intuitive for alot of our users, leading to high back navigation rate to the cart page, where the checkout summary is clearly visible, so we redesigned the checkout summary section to be clearly visible on the shipping page itself, which should reduce back navigation to the cart page, and leading to increased CR
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [April] Reduced back navigation but negatively impacted Conversion Rate (CR). | Explicit price breakdowns prior to payment likely triggered user hesitation. | Next steps: Test hidden dropdowns for checkout summary and placement (above/below payment methods).

remarks: Report pill: ↺ Iterate · Report months: April

title: Item cards — show exact rating number
hypothesis: Displaying the exact rating number on item cards reduces unnecessary browsing and increases ATC rate and purchase rate.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: ✓ Ship A · Report months: April+May · April status: ✗ Negative

title: ATC button Interaction change (English vs Bangla)
hypothesis: In mobile-veiw, clicking on "Add to cart" button changes the button text to "Added to cart" and subsequent clicks does not do anything. Changing the button text to "Go to Cart" / "কার্টে যান" so the next click navigates to our cart page. We tests both English and Bangla copy of the button text. (test implemented only for our superstore page)
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes:
[April] Low visibility. | Re-run on high-traffic/high-visibility pages to gather actionable data.
[May] Both language variants lift purchase rate strongly vs control — English +59% (19.5%) and Bangla +35% (16.6%) — suggesting the "Go to Cart" navigation removes a real friction point in the superstore add-to-cart flow. | Samples are small (~1.3–2.1K/arm) and not yet significant. Scale the Bangla variant (more traffic, +35% purchase lift) site-wide to confirm on full traffic before shipping. | Granular view — Goto-cart click flow Filtering to users who clicked the "Goto Cart" button (control has no such CTA, baseline = 0), Bangla drives more clicks and converts at over 2× the purchase rate of English. Both variants generate entirely net-new purchases vs control. Metric Control English Bangla Goto-cart clicked 0 + 9.4% (+125) + 10.5% (+217) Purchase (of exposed) 0 + 2.5% (+33 orders) + 5.3% (+109 orders) Bangla incremental: 109 orders in 54d → 61/month → ৳59,613/month | This test was designed to keep our mobile-view consistent with our app UI. Due to a technical errorshuffle, this test may have to be re-run if our UX team decides the change is not effective.

remarks: Report pill: ✓ Ship English · Report months: April+May · April status: ↗ Scale test

title: Item card — price font size increase
hypothesis: Price is a key scanning signal. For our desktop view, a proportionally larger price (14/16/18/20 px) font should increase CTR.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: Scale Test · Report months: April

title: Featured category — intent-driven title copy
hypothesis: A title that expresses the category's intent and value should increase CTR on this section.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes:
[April] Results are not statistically significant yet. Control and Variant C are trending very close to each other; the experiment needs to run longer to identify a clear winner.
[May] All three rewritten titles massively out-perform the control on engagement — CTR jumps from 3.1% to 16–20% and view-item from 2.1% to ~9–11%. An intent-driven, descriptive title clearly makes the section far more clickable. | Purchase counts are still tiny (5 → 36), so the bottom-funnel lift is directional only. Variant C (কার্টের একাডেমিক ক্যাটাগরি) leads on purchase rate (1.28%); roll it out and keep running to reach significance on purchases.

remarks: Report pill: ✓ Ship C · Report months: April+May · April status: ↻ Extend

title: Price-Layout-Simplification-Mobile-Product-Card

results.notes: [April] Even though the results are looking favorable for Variant B, the metrics have not reached statistical significance yet. The experiment needs to run for a longer period to confirm the lift.

hypothesis: Increasing the font size of the percentage discount text (14px to 16px/18px) to make it more prominent and drive ATC rates on mobile.

remarks: Report pill: ↻ Extend · Report months: April

title: Checkout — Order Summary Dropdown & Redesign
hypothesis: A previous test revealed that a highly visible order summary can induce purchase fear, increasing drop-off by 2%. This iteration hides the summary in an expandable dropdown, changes its placement, and separates the payment total/order summary button from the payment methods to reduce friction.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [May] Variant A (Dropdown Bottom) is the winner with a +1.4pp purchase-rate lift (68.6% vs 67.2%), projecting to ৳1,67,890/month on this high-traffic checkout. Variant C (Dropdown Top) showed a higher raw lift (+1.8pp) but Variant A is the selected ship based on overall test evaluation. | Traffic split is now balanced across arms (1,480–1,721 each), so the earlier SRM concern is resolved. Variants B and C are slightly under-allocated vs A — keep monitoring the split as the test continues. | 3-day runtime is below the 7-day minimum. Extend the run to a full week to confirm Variant C's lift before shipping.

remarks: Report pill: ✓ Ship A · Report months: April+May · April status: Code Review

title: Login — Improve OTP Option Visibility
hypothesis: Users increasingly prefer logging in via phone number and OTP over email/password. Currently, the OTP option requires an extra step. Exposing the OTP option immediately and updating placeholders to prioritize phone numbers will increase the login conversion rate.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: Not live · Report months: April+May · April status: QA Testing

title: Cart — Pop-up Login Modal
hypothesis: ~70% of users browse without logging in. The current redirect to a separate login page creates a context switch and friction that increases cart drop-off. Keeping users in-context with a login modal on the cart page will prevent this redirect.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: Not live · Report months: April+May · Report label: TEST-041 · April status: ● Blocked

title: Quick Deals — Urgency and Scarcity Labels
hypothesis: Quick Deal lists show urgency bars and stock availability, but lack strong scarcity warnings. Adding explicit scarcity labels will increase click-through rates and purchase intent for Quick Deal items.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: ● Re-implement · Report months: April+May · Report label: TEST-038 · April status: ● Re-implement

title: Cart — Quick Deal Stock Remaining
hypothesis: Cart pages currently do not show the remaining stock for Quick Deal items. Displaying the stock left amount within the cart will create urgency and increase the cart completion rate for these limited-time products.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: Not live · Report months: April+May · April status: Pending

title: PDP — Quick Deal Stock Remaining
hypothesis: Currently, Product Detail Pages for items in a Quick Deal do not show the remaining stock status. Adding this visual indicator will induce scarcity and urgency, encouraging users to complete their purchase faster.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [April] Investigated and submitted a detailed report regarding the login redirection malfunction originating from the cart page. View Report ↗ | Improved and optimized the server-side implementation of GrowthBook for more robust and reliable A/B test delivery, and a more maintainable code base. | Submitted comprehensive requirements for implementing embedded surveys to collect real, actionable user feedback directly within the platform. View Requirements ↗ | Collaborated with the SE and SQA team to utilize GrowthBook for safe, phased feature rollouts. Case Study: My Orders & Order Track Redesign We are currently using GrowthBook to cautiously roll out the redesigned my-orders and order-track flow to our OG users first. We will collect brief feedback on their experience, and if positive, expand the rollout to the rest of the user base. This approach significantly mitigates risk and ensures a smooth UX transition. If any major bug arises we are also able to turn-off the feature without requiring a revert and re-deployment from the dev-ops team. This in potential can reduce risk and potential points of stress for our SQA and dev-ops team. | Simply surfacing the extra-discount page in the main nav drove ×3.7 views and ৳80,269/month in incremental revenue — with no product changes. There are almost certainly other high-value, low-visibility pages that would respond the same way. Recommend a nav audit in May. | Fixing the "Confirm Order" button placement above the checkout summary (Exp 11) drove a +6.73% absolute purchase rate lift. Essential actions must never be buried below the fold, especially for new users without saved shipping data. | The Bangla "কার্টে যান" label drove +14.4% view-cart rate vs. +12.8% for English — a meaningful gap for a primarily Bengali-speaking audience. This pattern should be tested systematically across search, wishlist, and checkout CTAs in May. | The featured category title test (Variant C: +4.9% CTR) outperformed all design-only tests this month. Messaging that communicates clear value intent drives stronger engagement than visual styling alone. Prioritise copy testing in H2. | Scaling the "%OFF" discount font (Exp 12) drove a minor +0.53% purchase rate lift, while structural changes like CTA placement drove 10x that impact. We should balance fine-tuning UI details with high-leverage structural tests. | Multiple experiments ran for only 1–6 days — insufficient to achieve statistical significance or capture weekly traffic patterns. Going forward, all tests should have a minimum 7-day runtime, with a pre-set sample size calculation from GrowthBook before launch.

remarks: Report pill: Not live · Report months: April+May · April status: Pending

title: Express buy-now vs. regular checkout
hypothesis: Two parallel paths to purchase from the same visitor base. Buy-now leads on intent capture; its lower completion is expected, since many users tap it to preview their total cost.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [May] Buy-now captures more intent. 32,609 users click it — 3.05% of visitors, 1.6× the 20,038 who enter regular checkout. The express CTA clearly pulls people in. | Lower completion is expected, not lost intent. Many users tap buy-now to preview their total cost (with delivery) before deciding — so a good share of the 22.2% completion reflects price discovery. Even so, buy-now nets ~90% of regular checkout's purchases (7,241 vs 8,020) while serving that need. | Refinement: surface total cost earlier. Showing delivery + final price on the product/cart — before buy-now — means price-checkers don't need to enter the flow just to see it, converting more of those clicks into completed orders without hurting the preview use.

remarks: Report pill: ✓ Performing · Report months: May

title: You Saving Nudge — Cart & Checkout
hypothesis: Adding a "You're saving ৳X" message on cart and checkout reinforces perceived value and reduces abandonment before purchase.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [May] V1 improves checkout rate by +2.1pp on solid traffic (~9.7K/arm) — the savings nudge clearly motivates users to proceed to checkout. Purchase rate lift is marginal (+0.1pp); the gain leaks between checkout and purchase, so it needs more data to confirm bottom-funnel impact. | V2 shows no meaningful advantage over V1. V1 is the more promising arm; consider stopping V2 to consolidate traffic on V1 for faster significance.

remarks: Report pill: ✓ Ship English · Report months: May

title: Mobile Cart — Original Price Swap
hypothesis: On the mobile cart, swapping the position of original and discounted price makes savings more prominent and increases checkout motivation.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [May] Checkout rate is flat (40.0% both arms) but purchase rate edges up +0.4pp (16.2% → 16.6%) on healthy traffic (~2.5K/arm). A small, directionally positive bottom-funnel signal. | The +0.4pp purchase lift is well within noise at this sample size and not yet significant. Keep running to confirm before scaling the mobile cart price swap.

remarks: Report pill: ↻ Iterate · Report months: May

title: Gift Wrap — Redesign
hypothesis: Redesigning the gift wrap selection UI to be more visually prominent and easier to interact with, increasing gift wrap add-on rate and overall purchase confidence.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [May] Gift-wrap orders trend slowly — only ~140 orders per arm in the current window. The +2.1% lift (142 → 145) is directionally positive but well within normal variance at this volume. No meaningful call can be made yet. | Keep running until each arm reaches ~500+ gift-wrap orders. Given the current rate, this will require a longer observation window — likely spanning into peak gifting periods for a reliable signal.

remarks: Report pill: ↻ Iterate · Report months: May

title: Recently Sold Superstore Products — Title Update
hypothesis: Testing alternate title copy for the "Recently Sold" superstore section to improve click-through rate to individual product pages.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [May] Low traffic (~470–530/arm). Results are not statistically significant — directional only. | English lifts product click-through (+3.8pp) and Bangla lifts ATC (+2.9pp), but both drop end-purchase rate (−1.8pp / −0.8pp). The title change moves users into the funnel but doesn't convert — keep running before concluding. | Granular view — Mobile-only segment On the mobile-view segment (~190/arm, 7-day run) both variants win on purchase while the combined results above show losses. Bangla leads on every metric — worth shipping mobile-only or re-running at scale. Metric Control English Bangla Participants ~190 ~190 ~190 Product-viewed rate 48.5% + 0.0% + 2.1% ATC rate (of exposed) 16.7% + 0.0% + 7.4% (24.1%) Purchase rate (of exposed) 4.0% + 1.6% (5.6%) + 3.6% (7.6%) Bangla wins on mobile across all funnel stages — consider mobile-only ship or scaled re-run

remarks: Report pill: ↻ Iterate · Report months: May

The Bangla "View All" copy is essentially neutral on conversion across ~1.8K/arm. | V2 edges control on book-viewed (+0.4pp) and ATC (+0.4pp) but loses it back at purchase. No variant is worth shipping on current data — close as neutral or retest with a stronger treatment.

remarks: Report pill: ↻ Iterate · Report months: May

hypothesis: The quick-deal urgency and scarcity label test (TEST-038) was designed for desktop product cards. Mobile product cards have a different layout and smaller footprint. Adapting the badge design specifically for mobile will ensure the scarcity signal is visible and effective, increasing quick-deal conversion on mobile.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

title: Related Products — Quick Deal API Priority
hypothesis: The 'Related Products' section on PDP currently ranks items by generic similarity. Boosting quick-deal products to the top of the recommendations list will surface high-urgency, discounted items to users already in a buying context, increasing click-through to quick-deal PDPs and overall quick-deal conversion.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: QA Testing · Report months: May · Report label: TEST-055

title: Product Card — Stock Urgency Indicator
hypothesis: Product cards across PLP and search results currently show no stock-level information. Adding a 'Only X left' or 'Low stock' label on product cards for items with critically low inventory will create scarcity cues at the browse stage, increasing both ATC rate and purchase urgency before users even reach the PDP.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: QA Testing · Report months: May · Report label: TEST-056

title: Checkout — Streamlined Payment Section Copy
hypothesis: The checkout payment section contains redundant labels, verbose method descriptions, and repeated text that increases cognitive load. Reducing the copy to the minimum needed — short method names, no redundant sub-labels — will lower decision friction and improve payment step completion rate.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com
remarks: Report pill: QA Testing · Report months: May · Report label: TEST-057

title: Cart — Trust Badges Above Checkout CTA
hypothesis: The cart page jumps straight to the "Continue to Checkout" CTA with no reassurance at the moment of decision. Adding a row of trust badges — "100% Original Product", "7-Day Happy Return", and "Buy & Earn Points" — directly above the CTA should reduce purchase anxiety and lift checkout click-through.
creator: syed.nafis.sadique@gmail.com

results.notes: [May] Pulled and analysed order data segmented by SEO traffic source for May. Identified top-converting organic search entry points and flagged underperforming landing pages for CRO prioritisation. | Compiled a device-type breakdown report across sessions, add-to-cart, and purchase events. Findings used to prioritise mobile-first test design and flag desktop-specific friction points. | Analysed conversion rates across publisher landing pages — view-to-ATC and ATC-to-purchase funnels by publisher. Surfaced low-converting publishers with high traffic as prime targets for page-level CRO tests. | Planned the integration of Formbricks for in-product survey delivery. Completed initial testing of survey triggers, response collection, and data pipeline into the analytics stack. Groundwork for qualitative user research at scale. | Integrated GrowthBook's Visual Editor into the platform, enabling no-code experiment creation directly on live pages. Reduces reliance on developer time for front-end A/B test setup and expands the team's ability to run tests independently. | The Order Summary dropdown redesign (Test 3, Variant C) lifted purchase rate 67.2% → 68.6% on high-intent checkout traffic and projects to ৳1,67,890/month — the single largest win of the month. Optimising the bottom of the funnel, where users already intend to buy, returned more per visitor than any top-of-funnel acquisition tweak. Prioritise checkout and cart surfaces. | The Superstore title test was flat-to-negative on the full funnel (Bangla −0.7pp purchase) yet won decisively on mobile alone — Bangla +3.6pp purchase (7.6% vs 4.0%) and +7.4pp ATC. A single pooled number completely masked opposite behaviour across devices. Device-segmented analysis must become standard before every ship decision — this is the strongest signal of the month. | English won the cart ATC-interaction test (+7.2pp purchase, 12.3% → 19.5%) while Bangla won on the Superstore mobile segment. There is no fixed "best language" — it shifts by surface and by device. Bangla vs English should be A/B'd per placement rather than decided once and rolled out everywhere. | The featured-category title test (Variant C) lifted click-through from 3.1% → 17.9% — a ×5.8 jump — with no design change at all. Copy that signals clear value intent outperformed every design-only test this month and ships fast. Build a copy-testing backlog for high-traffic entry points. | Several experiments ran at ~1–2K participants/arm with too few purchases to reach statistical significance (Superstore, Boimela, Original-swap) — their lifts are directional, not confirmed. Validate promising winners by scaling them onto high-traffic surfaces before treating the revenue as banked. | Our biggest win (Order Summary, 4 days) reached volume fast because checkout traffic is dense, but shorter windows miss weekly cycles on lower-traffic surfaces. Hold low-traffic tests to a ≥7-day window with a pre-set GrowthBook sample-size target, and confirm short high-traffic wins over a full week before a 100% rollout. | This month's device split proved that audience cohorts respond in opposite directions to the same change. The same is almost certainly true geographically — Bangladesh's major cities (Dhaka, Chattogram, Sylhet, Khulna) differ in delivery speed, COD-vs-online payment mix and price sensitivity, none of which we have tested yet. June's roadmap formally adds device-type and city-level experiment cohorts to capture these differences and stop averaging away real wins.

remarks: Report pill: Not live · Report months: May
